flashcards.front: What does the CAP theorem state?
flashcards.back: A distributed system can only simultaneously guarantee two out of three: Consistency, Availability, and Partition Tolerance.

flashcards.front: What is a crash-stop failure model?
flashcards.back: Nodes fail by stopping permanently and do not recover.

flashcards.front: What distinguishes synchronous from asynchronous distributed systems?
flashcards.back: Synchronous systems have bounded communication delays, while asynchronous systems have no timing guarantees.
Ability to function correctly even if some nodes behave arbitrarily (maliciously).

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of logical clocks?
flashcards.back: To establish causal ordering of events without physical time synchronization.
All replicas will converge to the same state given no new updates.
Failure where nodes are split into disconnected subgroups.

flashcards.front: What is the two-phase commit protocol?
flashcards.back: Atomic commitment protocol using prepare/commit phases.

flashcards.front: What is a distributed system model?
flashcards.back: Formal abstraction describing components, interactions, and failure assumptions.

flashcards.front: What is the FLP impossibility result?
flashcards.back: Proves consensus is impossible in async systems with one faulty process.

flashcards.front: What distinguishes fail-stop from fail-recovery models?
flashcards.back: Fail-recovery allows nodes to restart after crashing.
Algorithm for capturing causal relationships using vector timestamps.
